messages: Would you recommend picking up IPA symbols early to get a handle on unusual sounds, or wait until the basics are solid?
Are there recent studies that show significant long-term cognitive benefits to maintaining bilingualism?
What common mistakes do learners make when tackling complex verb systems?
When setting goals for a year of study, what are realistic benchmarks for communicative competence instead of test scores?

Okay, planning question: given I only have a very short pre-commute slot on weekdays and the certification is scheduled for early autumn, what should that daily slot look like to produce steady gains?
Should those tiny weekday windows prioritize repetition of core grammar patterns, short production drills, or something else entirely?
A friend suggested I ditch the micro-sessions and instead do two long evening weekend blocks of 90-120 minutes; would that be better for hitting the certification goal?
If I stick with daily short bursts, how should I set spaced-repetition parameters—fewer new items per day, different intervals, or a different approach entirely?
Since I can only rely on listening during those moments, would an audio-first SRS plus short shadowing be the most efficient use of that slot, or should I use it for brief spoken output practice?
Given I don't want a pricey weekly subscription, is investing in a small number of monthly tutor hours a better use of funds, and if so roughly how many hours per month would be minimally effective?
What's one two-minute morning exercise that preserves pronunciation and rhythm when you have almost no time?
Is it realistic to expect to reach competent productive ability for a highly inflected language with mostly short, listening-centered weekday practice plus occasional longer sessions?
If the certification includes productive assessment, which tiny morning activities are non-negotiable so I don't arrive there with only receptive strengths?
How should I structure the rest of the week around those quick morning bursts so reading and writing skills don't fall completely behind, given my modest budget?
Could you sketch a 30-day micro-plan that fits into my weekday pre-commute minutes but maps to two-month checkpoints leading up to the certification?
Finally, if you could only pick three activities for the daily micro-slots, what would they be in order and why—and would adding two longer sessions change the priority?
